questions.question: Define Path Vector Routing and state how it differs from Distance Vector and Link State routing protocols.
keyPoints: Path Vector Routing maintains entire path information (sequence of AS numbers)
Distance Vector only maintains distance metric to each destination
Link State maintains complete topology map of the domain
Path Vector prevents routing loops by detecting own AS in path
Path Vector is suitable for inter-domain routing, DV for small networks, LS for intra-domain

questions.question: List and explain the four BGP message types with their purposes.
keyPoints: OPEN: Initializes BGP connection and exchanges capabilities
UPDATE: Advertises new routes or withdraws unavailable routes
KEEPALIVE: Maintains neighbor relationship periodically
NOTIFICATION: Reports errors and closes BGP connection

questions.question: Explain how AS-PATH attribute prevents routing loops in BGP.
keyPoints: AS-PATH contains ordered list of all AS numbers the route has traversed
When BGP receives an update, it checks if its own AS number is in AS-PATH
If own AS is found, it indicates a loop - route is discarded
Before re-advertising route, BGP prepends its own AS number to AS-PATH
This ensures no AS receives updates about destinations within its own domain

questions.question: Differentiate between eBGP and iBGP.
keyPoints: eBGP: External BGP - runs between routers in different Autonomous Systems
iBGP: Internal BGP - runs between routers within the same AS
eBGP typically uses direct physical connection; iBGP uses TCP (port 179)
iBGP requires full mesh or route reflectors; eBGP doesn't
NEXT-HOP attribute changed by eBGP but preserved by iBGP
Both use same message types and selection algorithm

questions.question: Explain the BGP Path Attributes: AS-PATH, NEXT-HOP, LOCAL-PREF, and MED.
keyPoints: AS-PATH: Sequence of AS numbers traversed; used for loop detection and route selection
NEXT-HOP: IP address of next-hop router to reach destination
LOCAL-PREF: Local preference for outbound traffic (higher preferred); default 100
MED: Multi-Exit Discriminator to influence inbound traffic (lower preferred)

questions.question: Describe the BGP route selection process criteria in order of preference.
keyPoints: 1. Check if route is valid (synchronized and reachable)
2. Highest LOCAL-PREF (default 100)
3. Shortest AS-PATH length
4. Lowest MED value
5. Prefer eBGP over iBGP routes
6. Lowest IGP cost to NEXT-HOP
7. Lowest BGP Router ID

questions.question: Explain the advantages and limitations of Path Vector Routing in inter-domain routing.
keyPoints: ADVANTAGES: Loop prevention via AS-PATH, support for policy-based routing, scalability for large networks, multiple path attributes for flexible routing decisions, hierarchical AS numbering enables aggregation

LIMITATIONS: Slow convergence due to path vector propagation, requires significant processing/memory for large AS-PATHs, complexity in configuration and policy implementation, AS-PATH length can grow very long, potential for routing policy conflicts between ASes

questions.question: Explain how policy-based routing is implemented in BGP and its importance in inter-domain routing.
keyPoints: BGP allows administrators to implement policies based on AS-PATH, community, or other attributes
Route filtering: Accept/reject routes based on AS-PATH prefix using prefix-lists or AS-path access-lists
Attribute manipulation: Modify LOCAL-PREF (outbound traffic), MED (inbound traffic)
Route summarization (aggregation) to reduce table size
Valley-free routing: Prevents traffic from one AS to another via a third AS without consent
Enables traffic engineering: Control which neighbor carries traffic for specific prefixes
Supports business relationships: Customer-provider, peer-peer routing policies

questions.question: Explain the BGP finite state machine states and transitions during neighbor establishment.
keyPoints: IDLE: Initial state; initiates TCP connection, resets ConnectRetry timer
CONNECT: Waiting for TCP connection completion; if fails, transitions to Active
ACTIVE: Continues trying to establish TCP connection
OPEN SENT: Waiting for OPEN message from neighbor
OPEN CONFIRM: Waiting for KEEPALIVE message; neighbor is established
ESTABLISHED: BGP connection active; exchanges UPDATE messages
State transitions triggered by: TCP connection success/failure, OPEN message receipt, KEEPALIVE/NOTIFICATION receipt, timer expiry, manual stop
